Snake Basin Steelhead Kelt Analysis

Ryan N. Kinzer1,✉, and Mike Ackerman1,✉

25 June, 2026

1 Nez Perce Tribe, Department of Fisheries Resources Management, 14054 Burr Dr., McCall, Idaho, 83638, USA

✉ Correspondence: Ryan N. Kinzer <ryank@nezperce.org>, Mike Ackerman <mikea@nezperce.org>

DRAFT

1 Data Sources

We evaluated kelting rates of natural-origin adult steelhead observed spawning in Snake River tributaries upstream of Lower Granite Dam (LGR) moving back downstream past LGR, simultaneously estimating the kelt detection probability of LGR. The dataset used in this analysis comprises of observations of PIT-tagged natural-origin adult steelhead (newly or previously tagged) trapped at the LGR adult ladder, beginning with spawn year 2010, and then subsequently detected near the spawning grounds at instream PIT-tag detection systems (IPTDS) or mark-recapture-recovery (MRR) sites. Post-spawn, PIT-tagged adults can additionally be “re-detected” moving downstream past LGR, at any downriver dam, or in the Columbia River estuary.

We generate the dataset by first compiling a list of PIT-tag observations for adult steelhead with valid PIT-tag codes collected at the LGR adult trap between July 1 and June 30 comprising each spawn year (Ogden 2020; Baum et al. 2023). All phenotypic natural-origin fish (i.e., present adipose fin and absence of a coded-wire tag) are included as part of the valid tag list. Phenotypic natural-orgin fish of hatchery-origin can later be identified using parentage-based tagging (Steele et al. 2013, 2019; Baum et al. 2023).

A PIT-tag code was considered valid if the adult steelhead was trapped while ascending the LGR fish ladder during a systematic subsampling period, had a fork length (FL) greater than 30 cm, and was not missing data for any of the following fields: date of collection, species, FL, origin (hatchery or wild), or adipose fin status (ad-clipped or ad-intact) within the LGR trapping database (Baum et al. 2023). Once the valid tag list was developed, we retrieved all PIT-tag interrogations for each code using the complete tag history query from PTAGIS. Observations were then processed using the statistical programming language R (R Core Team 2025) and the PITcleanr package (See et al. 2024). PIT-tag observations and data processing scripts are maintained within the SnakeRiverFishStatus GitHub repository.

The complete tag histories were initially processed using the PITcleanr::compress() function (See et al. 2024). This function, along with a supplied configuration file, collapsed multiple PIT-tag interrogations at the same location and within a similar time frame into a single record, retaining only essential information: the PIT-tag code, observation location (i.e., node), and the minimum and maximum timestamps of observations at that location (i.e., IPTDS, MRR site, or dam). For instance, all PIT-tag detection sites at Bonneville Dam (e.g., BCC, B2J, etc.) were merged into a single node, while LGR sites were categorized into a downstream migration node (GRJ, GRS) and an upstream migration node (GRA, LGRLDR, LGR). Similar to Bonneville Dam, all detection sites within a dam between LGR and Bonneville were merged into their own node. All detections upstream of LGR remained distinct to facilitate assignment of detections to specific spawning locations and identify movement direction. Finally, the dataset was truncated to include only observations occuring after an upstream-migrating steelhead was first detected at the LGR adult trap, excluding all juvenile and pre-return adult observations.

The resulting dataset consists of PIT-tag observations from a random sample of adult steelhead migrating past LGR and all subsequent detections of fish migrating during three distinct life stages: upstream towards spawning areas (spawners), downstream post-spawning (kelt), and returning upstream again through the hydrosystem (repeat spawners). A simple rule set was then applied to classify observations to locations and each life stage using functions maintained in the SnakeRiverKelt GitHub repository. The kelt stage was assigned to observations occurring at least five days after the last upstream spawner movement, observations indicating a downstram movement, and any detections at the lower Snake and Columbia river hydrosystems after April 20. The repeat spawner stage was assigned to all observations following the first upstream migration in any spawn year beyond the fish’s initial spawn year.

We additionally received a list of PIT-tagged adult steelhead kelts collected from the LGR juvenile bypass for the kelt reconditioning program starting in 2016 to 2021. Because these kelts are removed from the river for reconditioning, they were filtered from our evaluation of kelting and detection rates at LGR. The list consists of adults newly tagged at LGR and part of the valid list and previously tagged, which may or may not be within the valid tag list.

Finally, we refined our dataset to include only fish with a final designated species-run-rear (SRR) code of “32W” (i.e., steelhead, summer-run, wild). Hatchery-identified adults from the valid tag list were excluded, as they are not retained for natural-origin adult escapement analyses in the standard SnakeRiverFishStatus workflow, and the kelt reconditioning program focuses solely on natural-origin adults. Additionally, we removed a small number of fish with undetermined sex.

Kelts removed from the river for the reconditioning program were not included in our analysis of kelting and detection probability rates at LGR; however, their data were retained for comparison with all spawners observed as kelts at LGR and downstream.

In the 15 years of observation data, we have observed 16,273 PIT-tagged adult steelhead at Snake River spawning locations, after refining our dataset. Tables 1.1 and 1.2 show the number of these unique PIT-tagged spawners for each spawn year and within each Snake River population upstream of LGR. Additionally, the tables include the number of those spawners observed as kelt at LGR and downstream (i.e., below) of LGR.

The largest (i.e., greatest fork length) spawners typically occur in the Lochsa River (CRLOC-s), SF Salmon River (SFMAI-s), South Fork Clearwater River (CRSFC-s), Lolo Creek (CRLOL-s), Selway River (CRSEL-s), Sesech River (SFSEC-s), and Middle Fork Salmon River ((MFUMA-s and MFBIG-s) populations (Figure 1.1). The proportion of females returning to each population is lowest in the Little Salmon River (SRLSR-s) and upper mainstem Salmon River (SRUMA-s) populations and highest in the Pahsimeroi (SRPAH-s) and Panther Creek (SRPAN-s) populations (Figure 1.2).

Figure 1.1: Length distributions of steelhead sampled at Lower Granite Dam and later detected in spawning areas, all spawn years.

Figure 1.2: Distributions of the proportion of female steelhead, among spawn years, sampled at Lower Granite Dam and later detected in spawning areas.

2 Analysis

2.1 Reconditioned vs. All Kelts Comparison

We compared kelts collected by the reconditioning program in spawn year 2016 - 2021 to all kelts observed at LGR and/or at downstream hydropower systems to evaluate whether reconditioned kelts are representative of all kelts at-large (i.e., in-river migrating downstream past LGR). Figure 2.1 provides comparisons of size, run-timing (i.e., collection date at LGR during upstream migration), and proportions of genetic stocks and kelts previously observed at upstream spawning locations.

Figure 2.1: Comparison of all kelts observed at LGR or at downstream hydropower facilities (In River) to kelts collected by the reconditioning program (Reconditioned), spawn year 2016 - 2021, from those within the valid tag list. Comparisons include length, collection date at Lower Granite Dam from July 1, genetic stock proportions, and proportion of individuals observed at spawning sites.

2.2 Kelt Survival and Detection Modeling

We estimated post-spawn steelhead apparent survival (i.e., surviving and migrating) to LGR using a Cormack-Jolly-Seber (CJS) model (Lebreton et al. 1992). The likelihood function for an individual \(i\) in group \(g\) (e.g., a specific spawn year, population, and/or other covariate) follows:

\[ L = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(y_{it} | \phi^g, p^g) \]

where, \(y_{it} = 1\) if individual \(i\) is observed at time \(t\), and \(y_{it} = 0\) if the individual was not observed. \(\phi_t^g\) is the probability of survival from \(t\) to \(t+1\) for group \(g\), and \(p_t^g\) is the probability of detection at \(t\), given the individual is alive.

Since spawn year, population, and sex may explain variation in survival and detection probabilities, we incorporated these as fixed effects using a logit-link with the general model structure as (excluding possible interactions):

\[ \text{logit}(\phi_t) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 (\text{spawn year}) + \beta_2 (\text{population}) + \beta_3 (\text{sex}) \]

\[ \text{logit}(p_t) = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 (\text{spawn year}) + \alpha_2 (\text{population}) + \alpha_3 (\text{sex}) \]

where, \(\beta_0, \beta_1, \beta_2, \beta_3\) are coefficients for survival (\(\phi\)) and \(\alpha_0, \alpha_1, \alpha_2, \alpha_3\) are coefficients for detection (\(p\)). Biologically meaningful interaction terms were also included in candidate models; for example, spawn year X population and population x sex to examine if survival and detection probabilities vary differently across the two interaction terms.

We fit multiple candidate models with different variations of fixed effects and interactions, and then determined the best-fitting model using Akaike’s Information Criterion (AIC) (Akaike 1974) calculated as:

\[ AIC = -2 \log L + 2K \]

where, \(L\) is the likelihood of the model given the data and \(K\) is the number of estimated parameters. The model with the lowest AIC value was selected as the most parsimonious and best-fitting model.

All models were fit using the marked::cmr() (Laake et al. 2013) function in R (R Core Team 2025).

3 Results

The best fitting candidate model included terms for spawn year, population, and sex for estimating apparent survival and only spawn year for estimating detection probabilities (Table 3.1). Table 3.2 and Figures 3.1 and 3.2 show the estimate detection and survival probabilities for the best fitting model. Figure 3.3 and Table 3.3 provide average survival estimates conditioned on population and sex and marginalized over spawn years (Agresti 2003).

References

Agresti, A. 2003. Categorical data analysis. John Wiley & Sons.
Akaike, H. 1974. A new look at the statistical model identification. IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control 19(6):716–723.
Baum, C. M., J. S. Hargrove, A. C. Harris, M. Davison, M. E. Dobos, N. R. Smith, L. Chiaramonte, T. Copeland, and M. R. Campbell. 2023. Wild Adult Steelhead and Chinook Salmon Abundance and Composition at Lower Granite Dam, Spawn Year 2022 2022 Annual Report.
Laake, J. L., D. S. Johnson, and P. B. Conn. 2013. Marked: An R package for maximum likelihood and Markov Chain Monte Carlo analysis of capture–recapture data. Methods in Ecology and Evolution 4(9):885–890.
Lebreton, J.-D., K. P. Burnham, J. Clobert, and D. R. Anderson. 1992. Modeling Survival and Testing Biological Hypotheses Using Marked Animals: A Unified Approach with Case Studies. Ecological Monographs 62(1):67–118.
Ogden, D. 2020. Operation of the Adult Trap at Lower Granite Dam, 2020. BPA Project # 2005-002-00. Page 8.
R Core Team. 2025. R: A language and environment for statistical computing. R Foundation for Statistical Computing, Vienna, Austria.
See, K., R. N. Kinzer, R. Orme, and M. W. Ackerman. 2024. PITcleanr: Cleans up PIT tag capture histories. R package version 3.0.2, commit 8c432d402d587e3a02e8c3c5e0542ee8ff12429e.
Steele, C. A., E. C. Anderson, M. W. Ackerman, M. A. Hess, N. R. Campbell, S. R. Narum, and M. R. Campbell. 2013. A validation of parentage-based tagging using hatchery steelhead in the Snake River basin. Canadian Journal of Fisheries and Aquatic Sciences 70(7):1046–1054.
Steele, C. A., M. Hess, S. Narum, and M. Campbell. 2019. Parentage-Based Tagging: Reviewing the Implementation of a New Tool for an Old Problem. Fisheries 44(9):412–422.
